summary:
Phaistos was the second most important palatial center of [[event:53|Minoan Crete]], located on a prominent hill in the fertile Mesara plain of southern Crete. The palace complex at Phaistos was constructed and rebuilt over several centuries beginning around 1900 BC, reaching its fullest elaboration in the Neopalatial period between roughly 1700 and 1450 BC. Like the great palace at Knossos, Phaistos was organized around a large central court flanked by storerooms, administrative areas, residential quarters, and ceremonial spaces, following the characteristic Minoan palatial layout.

Phaistos is perhaps best known for the Phaistos Disc, a clay disk impressed with 241 signs arranged in a spiral pattern, discovered by Italian excavators in 1908. The disc, dated to around 1700 BC, has never been convincingly deciphered and remains one of the most famous undeciphered artifacts of the ancient world. The script impressed on the disc differs from both Linear A and Linear B, and its relationship to other Aegean writing systems is unclear.

The palace at Phaistos was destroyed around 1450 BC, at roughly the same time as most other Minoan palatial centers except Knossos. The cause of these destructions remains debated, with candidates including the aftermath of the [[event:1822|Minoan eruption of Thera]], Mycenaean conquest, internal revolt, or some combination of factors. Italian excavations at the site, begun in 1900, have continued with interruptions into the modern era and have produced important insights into Minoan administration, economy, and art.
